repr typically includes more information in its output than str. For built-in exceptions str will just return the exception message, while repr will also include the exception class name, which would be useful to me when debugging the issue. Since the error I mentioned above happens infrequently I want to gather as much information about the cause exception as possible.

For example:

```python
import ctypes
import ctypes.util
try:
    ctypes.CDLL(ctypes.util.find_library('c')).missing_function
except Exception as e:
  print 'str', str(e)
  print 'repr', repr(e)
```
outputs

```
/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: undefined symbol: missing_function
AttributeError('/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: undefined symbol: missing_function', )
```

C++11 has `unordered_map` and `unordered_set`. They are more resemblant of Python's set and dict as they provide O(1) access (as opposed to `map` and `set` which are treeish with O(log(N))).

You may have tried it and got an error. The problem is that C++ `std::hash()` isn't defined for `pair` objects. What you can do is hack the `__get_key()` to return an integer value, like this:
```cython
ctypedef long long key_t  # 64 bits should be enough for everyone

cdef inline key_t __get_key(int i, int j):
    return (<key_t>i << 32) + j
```
and then you have:
```cython
cdef unordered_map[key_t, CostEntry] D
```
which should work.

Good suggestion and example. But I'm not following the reasoning of the last sentence `Since the fork of a process replicates the complete process address space including threads, keeping MXNet single-threaded via the use of NaiveEngine makes it safe.`

The ThreadedEngine of MXNet schedules operations based upon the availability of NDArrays in their corresponding operation functions. The root cause for the problem we saw is that multiple threads pushes operations on NDArrays to the MXNet ThreadedEngine simultaneously. This will cause
1. data race, e.g. thread1 and thread2 writing to the same NDArray memory space.
2. dead lock, e.g. thread1 trying to copy from NDArray1 to NDArray2, while thread2 trying to copy from NDArray2 to NDArray1, and they are both waiting for the read on the source arrays ready.

If it's already multiple threads in a single process interacting with MXNet, does it matter by forking it or not?

1. My purpose is to report the loss value during training, this is because the loss value trends is helpful to monitor the convergence trend.

The current implementation of my PR is add a string-type argument, that is the "--loss", and the corresponding type loss will be reported accordingly.

Meanwhile, my understanding to Eric's comment is, it be better to set the the argument as a list including all the matching loss type (['ce', 'nll-loss'] for softmax), and give the user to decide which loss value will be used.

Hmm, yes -- that was my initial approach, but getting it to work with webpack-chain/Neutrino was nonobvious, although @eliperelman may have some ideas :). As I understand it, Neutrino sets up an empty commons chunk plugin and waits for you to add a vendor entrypoint like this that takes care of determining what will go into it. Using this mechanism, I don't think we can just do something like `reqiure('package.json').dependencies' to get a list of the right modules, since these `add(thing)` calls expect to find a javascript file eventually -- this means that for libs like bootstrap, which provide both css and js and have no default export, we're missing the right path for the js export by default (fontawesome was also a problem here because it's not even js).

I think another approach I could try is removing the default commons chunk plugin altogether and setting up another one based on the implicit scheme. I will give it a try today!

These acceptance tests need to run to an error driven by missing implementation. That means all the imports need to work and the test file loads as well (and syntax etc.). I've changed this to use an existing test file because any section will do, so no new file this time around. * A bit further to that point, you will need to be running these tests before submission. Firstly, you'll need to work out any errors in the test code itself. Second, you'll need the "actual" error it produces to know what to implement next; Section.header in this case because the error is 'AttributeError: 'Section' object has no attribute 'header''. You'll want to be seeing that error for yourself each time.

Coverage is run with three different config files,
+ pytest      -  .coveragerc.pytest
+ ctest        -  {build}/girder_worker/core/tests/girder_worker.coveragerc
+ combine  -  no config file (i.e. defaults)

the pytest coverage ignores all files in girder_worker/core/ and girder_worker/plugins/. If you try and combine the pytest coverage (which ends up in .coverage.pytest)  with the ctest coverage (which ends up in .coverage.legacy) with the .coveragerc.pytest config file,  then it ignores everything the ctest coverage created.  

Eventually .coveragerc.pytest will become .coveragerc,  but not until core and plugins are transitioned out of girder_worker.

Following the life cycle of this data structures is a little complex. In particular, ``registered_collections`` is filled on initialization, while the nodes start the structure is being constructed. On the other hand, ``collection`` acts as a Boolean to say that both the collection of tests in all nodes was completed and that the initial distribution of work as been done. You cannot overload the semantics of both into one.

In the particular line of code that you commented on we can change ``worker_collection = self.registered_collections[node]`` with ``self.collection`` without problem, both will be equivalent, but I preferred to grab the index from the node itself for correctness and to improve understanding that this needs to happen after the node was registered (independently if it was registered on initialization of later).

> I think you can change self.collection into a dict of suite -> list of item ids.

We could, but because of what I wrote in the first paragraph you will still need a structure to build while initializing, and a second one / boolean to indicate initialization is done, and if you look there are other utilitarian uses of ``self.collection`` that justify its existence.

I'm curious to know how you're thinking of implementing the pagination tokens, and I'm a little concerned that this won't quite be enough to make the pagination behave in ways that users expect.  A single token is good enough for an API case where the data is just one giant stream being consumed by an API client.  But in an actual user's pagination use cases, they will often want to navigate backwards as well as forwards, and potentially jump to arbitrary pages in the result set (i.e. jump to first page, last page, etc.).

Navigating backwards could be solved by keeping the old page data cached in the frontend (I think we have to cache it, since otherwise the query may have changed, resulting in pages that don't match what the user saw before, and potentially leading to missing results and/or incorrect page numbering.)  But jumping ahead in the result set is quite challenging I think, since if we don't cache all the intervening pages, navigating backwards might be broken (for the same reasons as just described), but if we do, this makes jumping to the last page equivalent to loading the entire result set which sort of defeats the point of paginating at the server side.

Maybe the right answer is just to not support any pagination options other than next page and previous page, and if the user wants to "jump to last" they have to manually reverse the sort order (it may be nice to provide a specific button for this that toggles the order on all active sorting filters).  Users may find this pretty annoying, but that might be acceptable if we enable it only for very high result set sizes (say 2000+ results).

Okay, I just looked back and saw:

> One thing that might be circleci specific is that the git submodule folder won't be available in the artman container running in circleci. To make it work, I add a git clone step in the golden-test circleci config, which is even better than a locked submodule folder, because that helps capture the potential regression caused by upstream common proto/config changes.

First of all, that is not CircleCI specific. `git clone` does not clone submodules by default. You have to provide `--recurse-submodules` or use `git submodule update` later. (This is an _extremely_ common misunderstanding of how submodules work.)

More importantly, you do not want a submodule. :-) You want to track HEAD. Kill the submodule and check out to a separate location.

The lousy part of this is that it is going to make it somewhat ornery to run the test locally. My recommendation would be to set it up with the following logic:

  1. If `CIRCLECI` is set, check `googleapis` out to a fixed location.
  2. Else, if `GOOGLEAPIS_DIR` is set, skip checkout entirely and just use whatever is there.
  3. Else, `pytest.skip`.
